title: Prospective associations of sleep duration and screen time with transition from overweight/obesity to normal BMI in U.S. adolescents.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Shorter sleep duration and longer screen time are established risk factors for adolescent obesity. However, the extent to which these behaviors are prospectively associated with the transition back from overweight/obesity to a healthy status remains unclear. We examined whether sleep duration and screen time among adolescents with overweight/obesity are associated with the likelihood of transitioning to a normal body mass index (BMI). METHODS: We used data from 3498 U.S. adolescents aged 9-11 years with overweight/obesity (45.1% female), from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Cox proportional hazards models examined the prospective associations of parent-reported sleep duration (9-11, 8-9, 7-8, and <7âh/day) and screen time (hours/day) with a shift from overweight/obesity (BMI percentile â¥85) to a normal (BMI percentile <85) accounting for key covariates including pubertal status. RESULTS: Over a median 657 days of follow-up, 643 (18.4%) adolescents transitioned from overweight/obesity to a normal BMI percentile. Compared with those sleeping 9-11âh, adolescents sleeping 7-8âh were less likely to transition to a normal BMI percentile (hazard ratio [HR]: 0.60, 95% CI 0.44, 0.82), with significant dose-response trend (p for trendâ=â0.003). The association remained significant in sex-stratified analyses for both females (HR: 0.55, 95% CI 0.30, 0.98) and males (HR: 0.59, 95% CI 0.41, 0.86), with similar significant trend in both groups (p for trendâ<0.05). Higher screen time was not associated with transitioning to a normal BMI overall (HR: 0.99, 95% CI 0.96, 1.02) or by sex (females, HR: 1.00, 95% CI 0.95, 1.05; males, HR: 0.99, 95% CI 0.95, 1.02). CONCLUSION: Short sleep duration was prospectively associated with a lower likelihood of transitioning to a normal BMI among adolescents with overweight/obesity. This association warrants further investigation as a potential intervention target.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 3498 U.S. adolescents aged 9-11 years from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as generic total composite (hours/day) from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey covering TV, videos, games, texting, video chat, social media combined. No separately reported phone/SM coefficient. Violates FT-IC2 requirement for specific smartphone OR social media variable.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Obesity, 2025

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; does not reach criteria check for FT-EC7

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data with 3498 adolescents aged 9-11 years from baseline, Year 1, and Year 2 assessments

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time variable from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey reporting average daily hours spent on TV, YouTube, video games, texting, video chatting, and social media

FT_IC3.evidence: Cox proportional hazards regression models examining prospective associations of screen time (continuous and categorical) with transition from overweight/obesity to normal BMI percentile

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: screen time continuous HR: 0.99 (95% CI 0.96, 1.02) overall; HR: 1.00 (95% CI 0.95, 1.05) females; HR: 0.99 (95% CI 0.95, 1.02) males

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal empirical analysis using Cox proportional hazards regression models with median 657 days follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Obesity, 2025

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (exposure), not the dependent variable. Paper examines associations of screen time with BMI transition (BMI is outcome). FT-EC7 applies only when screen time is the dependent variable predicted by demographics.

title: Social epidemiology of early adolescent problematic screen use in the United States.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To determine sociodemographic correlates of problematic screen use (social media, video games, mobile phones) among a racially/ethnically and socioeconomically diverse population-based sample of 10-14-year-old early adolescents. STUDY DESIGN: We analyzed cross-sectional data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Year 2, 2018-2020; Nâ=â8753). Multiple linear regression analyses were used to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parental education) and adolescent-reported problematic video game (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), social media (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and mobile phone use (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). RESULTS: Boys reported higher problematic video game use while girls reported higher problematic social media and mobile phone use. Native American, black, and Latinx adolescents reported higher scores across all problematic screen measures compared to non-Latinx white adolescents. Having unmarried/unpartnered parents was associated with higher problematic social media use. Although higher household income was generally protective against problematic video game use, these associations were weaker for black than white adolescents (p for interaction <0.05). CONCLUSIONS: Given the sociodemographic differences in problematic screen use, digital literacy education strategies can focus on at-risk populations, encourage targeted counseling by pediatricians, and adapt family media use plans for diverse backgrounds. IMPACT: While sociodemographic differences in screen time are documented, we examined sociodemographic differences in problematic screen use in a large, diverse sample of early adolescents in the US. Boys reported higher problematic video game use while girls reported higher problematic social media and mobile phone use. Native American, black, and Latinx adolescents reported higher scores across all problematic screen measures compared to non-Latinx white adolescents. Although higher household income was generally protective against problematic video game use, these associations were weaker for black than white adolescents. Beyond time spent on screens, pediatricians, parents, and educators should be aware of sociodemographic differences in problematic screen use.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 2 follow-up (2018-2020), N=8753 children aged 10-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three specific SM/phone variables: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking problematic phone/SM use to sociodemographic factors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental education). Table 3 reports associations with all three screen use measures

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for all associations (e.g., Male vs Female: B=0.74, 95% CI 0.69-0.79, p<0.001 for video games)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data with hypothesis testing and reported associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Pediatric Research, 2022); no evidence of duplicate/preprint

C1.FT_EC7_reason: APPLIES. Phone/SM variables are the DEPENDENT variables in all reported analyses (problematic video game use, social media use, mobile phone use are outcomes). Predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual (age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental education, parental marital status). Analysis is cross-sectional (not longitudinal).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study Year 2 data (2018-2020), N=8753 adolescents aged 10-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three specific smartphone/social media variables: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between sociodemographic factors and problematic screen use across three modalities

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports β coefficients with 95% confidence intervals (e.g., males vs females: B = 0.74 (0.69 to 0.79) for video games, p<0.001; B = -0.11 (-0.16 to -0.05) for social media, p<0.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Study using multiple linear regression to estimate associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatric Research (2022), no duplicate evidence

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design. FT-EC7 applies only when ALL analyses are longitudinal/prospective; cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as DV are excluded from FT-EC7 (ignores direction)

title: Generalizable prediction of childhood ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing and youth characteristics.
abstract: Childhood attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) symptoms are believed to result from disrupted neurocognitive development. However, evidence for the clinical and predictive value of neurocognitive assessments in this context has been mixed, and there have been no large-scale efforts to quantify their potential for use in generalizable models that predict individuals' ADHD symptoms in new data. Using data drawn from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD), a consortium that recruited a diverse sample of over 10,000 youth (ages 9-10 at baseline) across 21 U.S. sites, we develop and test cross-validated machine learning models for predicting youths' ADHD symptoms using neurocognitive abilities, demographics, and child and family characteristics. Models used baseline demographic and biometric measures, geocoded neighborhood data, youth reports of child and family characteristics, and neurocognitive tests to predict parent- and teacher-reported ADHD symptoms at the 1-year and 2-year follow-up time points. Predictive models explained 15-20% of the variance in 1-year ADHD symptoms for ABCD Study sites that were left out of the model-fitting process and 12-13% of the variance in 2-year ADHD symptoms. Models displayed high generalizability across study sites and trivial loss of predictive power when transferred from training data to left-out data. Features from multiple domains contributed meaningfully to prediction, including neurocognition, sex, self-reported impulsivity, parental monitoring, and screen time. This work quantifies the information value of neurocognitive abilities and other child characteristics for predicting ADHD symptoms and provides a foundational method for predicting individual youths' symptoms in new data across contexts.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (baseline ages 9-10) from 10,000+ youth across 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Includes screen time as predictor variable in machine learning models; measured from ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire and featured in predictive feature importance analysis (appears in continuous and categorical feature weights across PCR/LASSO models)

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning regression models (PCR and LASSO) with reported correlation coefficients between predicted and actual ADHD symptoms; LME models with fixed effects of SMA groups and coefficients reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports multiple r values and 95% CIs across models and timepoints (e.g., 1-year full sample PCR r=0.40, CI 0.38-0.42; LASSO r=0.39, CI 0.37-0.41; 2-year full sample PCR r=0.40, CI 0.38-0.42)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning predictive modeling with cross-validation across ABCD sites and left-out test data validation

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is a predictor (IV) of ADHD symptoms (DV), not the dependent variable. FT_EC7 only applies when smartphone/SM is the DV. Paper uses screen time among multiple predictors including neurocognitive, demographic, and child self-report features.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data from 21 U.S. sites; consortium with over 10,000 youth enrolled at baseline (ages 9-10)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time mentioned as one predictor among many in machine learning models (included in feature weights); no separately reported coefficient or analysis isolating smartphone/SM association; focus is neurocognition/demographics predicting ADHD, not smartphone/SM as primary exposure

FT_IC3.evidence: Screen time included only as predictor in multi-domain ML model; no inferential test or coefficient reported specifically linking screen time to non-SM outcome independent of other predictors

FT_IC4.evidence: No β/OR/HR/r/CI reported for smartphone/SM association

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: machine learning (PCR and LASSO) models predicting ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive, demographic, and child/family characteristics with cross-validation across ABCD sites

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Translational Psychiatry, 2023

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Fails FT_IC2 (screen time not separately analyzed with reported coefficient)
